title: Compose Advanced
description: LazyColumn, side effects, animations, and performance

references.title: Android Developers — Side Effects in Compose

lesson:
Building Custom Composables

Production Compose code demands reusable, well-architected composable components. The three pillars of a good composable API are: Modifier acceptance (callers control layout/behavior), state hoisting (composable is stateless; caller owns state), and slot APIs (callers inject content via lambdas).

Patterns for Reusable Composables

// PATTERN 1: Accept Modifier parameter (always first optional param) @Composable fun InfoCard( title: String, subtitle: String, modifier: Modifier = Modifier // callers can add padding, size, click, etc. ) { Card(modifier = modifier) { // pass modifier to root element Column(Modifier.padding(16.dp)) { Text(title, style = MaterialTheme.typography.titleMedium) Text(subtitle, style = MaterialTheme.typography.bodySmall) } } } // PATTERN 2: State hoisting — composable is stateless @Composable fun QuantitySelector( quantity: Int, // state flows DOWN onQuantityChange: (Int) -> Unit, // events flow UP modifier: Modifier = Modifier ) { Row(modifier = modifier, verticalAlignment = Alignment.CenterVertically) { IconButton(onClick = { onQuantityChange(quantity - 1) }) { Icon(Icons.Default.Remove, "Decrease") } Text("$quantity", style = MaterialTheme.typography.titleLarge) IconButton(onClick = { onQuantityChange(quantity + 1) }) { Icon(Icons.Default.Add, "Increase") } } } // Usage — caller owns the state var qty by remember { mutableIntStateOf(1) } QuantitySelector( quantity = qty, onQuantityChange = { qty = it.coerceIn(1, 99) }, modifier = Modifier.padding(8.dp) )
State hoisting makes composables testable, previewable, and reusable. The composable becomes a pure function of its parameters. The caller decides where state lives (ViewModel, remember, etc.) and how events are handled.

Slot-Based APIs

Slot APIs let callers inject composable content via lambda parameters. This is how Material 3 components like Scaffold, TopAppBar, and AlertDialog work — they define where content goes, and the caller defines what the content is.

// Scaffold uses slot APIs: topBar, bottomBar, floatingActionButton, content Scaffold( topBar = { TopAppBar(title = { Text("Home") }) }, bottomBar = { NavigationBar { /* items */ } }, floatingActionButton = { FloatingActionButton(onClick = {}) { Icon(Icons.Default.Add, null) } } ) { innerPadding -> // Main content slot — receives padding from Scaffold LazyColumn(contentPadding = innerPadding) { /* items */ } } // BUILDING YOUR OWN slot-based composable: @Composable fun DetailPageLayout( modifier: Modifier = Modifier, headerImage: @Composable () -> Unit, // slot: caller provides any image composable title: @Composable () -> Unit, // slot: caller provides styled text actions: @Composable RowScope.() -> Unit = {}, // slot with RowScope receiver content: @Composable ColumnScope.() -> Unit // slot: main content ) { Column(modifier = modifier.verticalScroll(rememberScrollState())) { Box(Modifier.fillMaxWidth().height(240.dp)) { headerImage() } title() Row( Modifier.fillMaxWidth().padding(horizontal = 16.dp), horizontalArrangement = Arrangement.End ) { actions() // caller can put any buttons here } content() // main body content } } // Usage: DetailPageLayout( headerImage = { AsyncImage(model = url, contentDescription = null, contentScale = ContentScale.Crop) }, title = { Text("Mountain Retreat", style = MaterialTheme.typography.headlineMedium, modifier = Modifier.padding(16.dp)) }, actions = { IconButton(onClick = { /* share */ }) { Icon(Icons.Default.Share, "Share") } IconButton(onClick = { /* favorite */ }) { Icon(Icons.Default.Favorite, "Favorite") } } ) { Text("A beautiful mountain retreat...", Modifier.padding(16.dp)) }
Slot API best practices:
1. Give slots descriptive names — headerImage not slot1.
2. Provide default values for optional slots: actions: @Composable () -> Unit = {}.
3. Use scope receivers (RowScope, ColumnScope) when the slot content should behave like children of a specific layout.
4. The content slot is conventionally the last parameter (trailing lambda syntax).

Custom Layout

When standard Row/Column/Box are not enough, Compose lets you build custom layouts from scratch using the Layout composable. You control exactly how children are measured and placed.

Custom Layout Pipeline: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ 1. Layout receives Constraints from parent (min/max width, min/max height) 2. Measure each child (measurable) with appropriate constraints → produces Placeables 3. Determine the layout size 4. Place each Placeable at (x, y) coordinates Key types: Measurable → a child that can be measured (call .measure(constraints)) Placeable → a measured child with known width/height (call .placeRelative(x, y)) Constraints → min/max width and height from parent MeasureScope → provides the layout() call to set size and place children
// Custom Layout: OverlappingRow — children overlap by a fixed amount @Composable fun OverlappingRow( overlapPx: Int, modifier: Modifier = Modifier, content: @Composable () -> Unit ) { Layout( content = content, modifier = modifier ) { measurables, constraints -> // Step 1: Measure all children with the incoming constraints val placeables = measurables.map { it.measure(constraints) } // Step 2: Calculate total width accounting for overlap val totalWidth = if (placeables.isEmpty()) 0 else placeables.sumOf { it.width } - overlapPx * (placeables.size - 1) val maxHeight = placeables.maxOfOrNull { it.height } ?: 0 // Step 3: Set the layout size and place children layout(totalWidth.coerceAtLeast(0), maxHeight) { var xOffset = 0 placeables.forEach { placeable -> placeable.placeRelative(xOffset, 0) xOffset += placeable.width - overlapPx } } } } // Usage: avatar stack with overlapping circles OverlappingRow(overlapPx = with(LocalDensity.current) { 16.dp.roundToPx() }) { avatarUrls.forEach { url -> AsyncImage( model = url, contentDescription = null, modifier = Modifier.size(48.dp).clip(CircleShape).border(2.dp, Color.White, CircleShape) ) } } // Custom Layout: Simple Staggered Grid @Composable fun StaggeredGrid( columns: Int, modifier: Modifier = Modifier, content: @Composable () -> Unit ) { Layout(content = content, modifier = modifier) { measurables, constraints -> val columnWidth = constraints.maxWidth / columns val childConstraints = constraints.copy( minWidth = 0, maxWidth = columnWidth ) // Measure children with column-width constraints val placeables = measurables.map { it.measure(childConstraints) } // Track the height of each column val columnHeights = IntArray(columns) // Assign each item to the shortest column val itemPositions = placeables.map { placeable -> val col = columnHeights.indexOfMin() val x = col * columnWidth val y = columnHeights[col] columnHeights[col] += placeable.height IntOffset(x, y) } layout(constraints.maxWidth, columnHeights.max()) { placeables.forEachIndexed { i, placeable -> placeable.placeRelative(itemPositions[i].x, itemPositions[i].y) } } } } // Helper extension private fun IntArray.indexOfMin(): Int { var minIndex = 0 forEachIndexed { i, v -> if (v < this[minIndex]) minIndex = i } return minIndex }
Layout rules: Each child must be measured exactly once (calling measure() twice on the same measurable throws an exception). If you need to measure a child with different constraints, use SubcomposeLayout which allows measuring in multiple passes. The IntrinsicMeasurable interface provides minIntrinsicWidth/maxIntrinsicWidth for lookahead measurement without the single-measure restriction.

Custom Drawing — Canvas and DrawScope

The Canvas composable gives you a DrawScope where you can draw shapes, paths, text, and images directly. It is the Compose equivalent of Android's View.onDraw(Canvas).

// Basic Canvas drawing Canvas(modifier = Modifier.fillMaxWidth().height(200.dp)) { val w = size.width val h = size.height // Draw a filled rectangle drawRect( color = Color.Blue, topLeft = Offset(0f, 0f), size = Size(w * 0.5f, h) ) // Draw a circle with gradient brush drawCircle( brush = Brush.radialGradient( colors = listOf(Color.Yellow, Color.Red), center = center, radius = h * 0.4f ), radius = h * 0.4f, center = center ) // Draw a line drawLine( color = Color.White, start = Offset(0f, h / 2), end = Offset(w, h / 2), strokeWidth = 4.dp.toPx(), cap = StrokeCap.Round ) // Draw an arc (quarter pie) drawArc( color = Color.Green, startAngle = 0f, sweepAngle = 90f, useCenter = true, topLeft = Offset(w * 0.6f, h * 0.1f), size = Size(h * 0.8f, h * 0.8f) ) } // Drawing a custom path (triangle) Canvas(modifier = Modifier.size(100.dp)) { val path = Path().apply { moveTo(size.width / 2, 0f) // top center lineTo(size.width, size.height) // bottom right lineTo(0f, size.height) // bottom left close() } drawPath( path = path, brush = Brush.linearGradient(listOf(Color.Magenta, Color.Cyan)), style = Fill // or Stroke(width = 4.dp.toPx()) ) } // Drawing text on Canvas (requires TextMeasurer) val textMeasurer = rememberTextMeasurer() Canvas(modifier = Modifier.fillMaxWidth().height(60.dp)) { val result = textMeasurer.measure( text = AnnotatedString("Canvas Text"), style = TextStyle(fontSize = 24.sp, color = Color.Black) ) drawText(result, topLeft = Offset(16.dp.toPx(), 16.dp.toPx())) }
DrawScope Quick Reference: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ drawRect(color/brush, topLeft, size) — rectangle drawCircle(color/brush, radius, center) — circle drawOval(color/brush, topLeft, size) — oval/ellipse drawLine(color, start, end, strokeWidth) — line segment drawArc(color, startAngle, sweepAngle) — arc or pie slice drawPath(path, color/brush, style) — arbitrary path drawPoints(points, pointMode, color) — point set drawImage(imageBitmap, topLeft) — bitmap drawText(textLayoutResult, topLeft) — text (needs TextMeasurer) Brush types: SolidColor(color) — flat fill Brush.linearGradient(...) — linear gradient Brush.radialGradient(...) — radial gradient Brush.sweepGradient(...) — sweep/conic gradient Style: Fill (default) — filled shape Stroke(width, cap, join) — outlined shape
Modifier.drawBehind / Modifier.drawWithContent: You don't need a standalone Canvas for simple drawing. Use Modifier.drawBehind { drawCircle(...) } to draw behind the content of any composable, or Modifier.drawWithContent { drawContent(); drawCircle(...) } to draw both behind and on top.

Custom Modifiers — Modifier.Node API

When the built-in modifiers are not enough, you can create your own. The modern approach (Compose 1.3+) uses Modifier.Node, which replaces the older composed { } pattern. Modifier.Node is more efficient because it avoids per-composition allocations.

// Custom modifier: draws a colored circle indicator in the top-right corner // Step 1: Define the Modifier.Node (holds state, performs the effect) class CircleIndicatorNode( var color: Color, var radius: Float ) : DrawModifierNode, Modifier.Node() { override fun ContentDrawScope.draw() { drawContent() // draw the composable's content first drawCircle( color = color, radius = radius, center = Offset(size.width - radius - 4.dp.toPx(), radius + 4.dp.toPx()) ) } } // Step 2: Define the ModifierNodeElement (factory + update logic) data class CircleIndicatorElement( val color: Color, val radius: Float ) : ModifierNodeElement<CircleIndicatorNode>() { override fun create() = CircleIndicatorNode(color, radius) override fun update(node: CircleIndicatorNode) { node.color = color // update existing node — avoids reallocation node.radius = radius } override fun InspectorInfo.inspectableProperties() { name = "circleIndicator" properties["color"] = color properties["radius"] = radius } } // Step 3: Extension function on Modifier for clean API fun Modifier.circleIndicator(color: Color, radius: Dp = 6.dp): Modifier = this then CircleIndicatorElement(color, with(/* density needed */) { radius.toPx() }) // Usage: Icon( Icons.Default.Notifications, "Notifications", modifier = Modifier.size(32.dp).circleIndicator(Color.Red) )
Modifier.Node Architecture: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ModifierNodeElement → immutable description (data class), handles create/update creates → Modifier.Node → mutable stateful object, reused across recompositions implements → Node interfaces: DrawModifierNode → custom drawing (ContentDrawScope.draw) LayoutModifierNode → custom measurement/placement (MeasureScope.measure) PointerInputModifierNode → gesture handling SemanticsModifierNode → accessibility GlobalPositionAwareModifierNode → position callbacks Why Modifier.Node over composed {}? composed {} creates new instances every recomposition Modifier.Node reuses the node, only calling update() when params change → less allocation, better performance, easier debugging

Side Effects — The Complete Reference

Side effects are operations that escape the scope of a composable function: network calls, logging, writing to a database, showing a toast. Compose provides specific APIs for each pattern:

APIWhen It RunsUse ForProvides Cleanup?
LaunchedEffect(key)On enter + when key changesOne-shot or keyed coroutines (fetch data, show snackbar)Cancelled automatically
DisposableEffect(key)On enter + when key changesRegister/unregister patterns (observers, listeners, callbacks)Yes — onDispose { }
SideEffectAfter every successful recompositionSync Compose state to non-Compose code (analytics, logging)No
produceState(initial)On enter (creates State<T>)Convert non-Compose async sources (Flow, callback) to Compose StateCancelled automatically
derivedStateOf { }When source state changesComputed state that only recomposes when result changesN/A
rememberUpdatedStateCaptures latest valueReferencing latest callback/value inside long-running effectsN/A
rememberCoroutineScope()Returns scope for event handlersLaunch coroutines from callbacks (onClick, etc.)Cancelled on leave

LaunchedEffect — Keyed Coroutines

// KEY = Unit → runs ONCE when composable enters composition LaunchedEffect(Unit) { viewModel.loadData() } // KEY = userId → cancels + re-launches when userId changes LaunchedEffect(userId) { val user = api.fetchUser(userId) // cancelled + restarted on new userId userName = user.name } // Snackbar example (showSnackbar is a suspend function) @Composable fun ErrorSnackbar(error: String?, snackbarHostState: SnackbarHostState) { LaunchedEffect(error) { error?.let { val result = snackbarHostState.showSnackbar( message = it, actionLabel = "Retry", duration = SnackbarDuration.Short ) if (result == SnackbarResult.ActionPerformed) { // user tapped "Retry" } } } } // Navigation example: navigate based on one-time event LaunchedEffect(Unit) { viewModel.navigationEvent.collect { destination -> navController.navigate(destination) } }
Key behavior matters!
- LaunchedEffect(Unit) — runs once, never re-launches (lifecycle = composable's presence).
- LaunchedEffect(someState) — cancels the previous coroutine and re-launches when someState changes.
- Multiple keys: LaunchedEffect(key1, key2) — re-launches if either key changes.

DisposableEffect — Setup + Cleanup

// Register a lifecycle observer — MUST clean up DisposableEffect(lifecycleOwner) { val observer = LifecycleEventObserver { _, event -> if (event == Lifecycle.Event.ON_RESUME) isActive = true if (event == Lifecycle.Event.ON_PAUSE) isActive = false } lifecycleOwner.lifecycle.addObserver(observer) onDispose { lifecycleOwner.lifecycle.removeObserver(observer) // cleanup! } } // Register a sensor listener DisposableEffect(Unit) { val sensorManager = context.getSystemService<SensorManager>()!! val accelerometer = sensorManager.getDefaultSensor(Sensor.TYPE_ACCELEROMETER)!! val listener = object : SensorEventListener { override fun onSensorChanged(event: SensorEvent) { acceleration = event.values[0] } override fun onAccuracyChanged(sensor: Sensor, accuracy: Int) {} } sensorManager.registerListener(listener, accelerometer, SensorManager.SENSOR_DELAY_UI) onDispose { sensorManager.unregisterListener(listener) // MUST unregister } } // Register a BroadcastReceiver DisposableEffect(Unit) { val receiver = object : BroadcastReceiver() { override fun onReceive(ctx: Context, intent: Intent) { batteryLevel = intent.getIntExtra(BatteryManager.EXTRA_LEVEL, -1) } } context.registerReceiver(receiver, IntentFilter(Intent.ACTION_BATTERY_CHANGED)) onDispose { context.unregisterReceiver(receiver) } }

SideEffect — Non-Suspend, Every Recomposition

// SideEffect runs after EVERY successful recomposition. // No coroutine scope. No cleanup. No key. Just a synchronous block. // Use case: syncing Compose state to non-Compose systems @Composable fun AnalyticsScreen(screenName: String) { SideEffect { // Called after every successful recomposition analytics.setCurrentScreen(screenName) } // ... screen content } // Syncing state to a non-Compose object @Composable fun MapComposable(location: LatLng) { val mapRef = remember { MapController() } SideEffect { // Always keep mapRef in sync with latest location mapRef.setCenter(location) } AndroidView(factory = { mapRef.createView(it) }) }

produceState — Convert Callbacks to Compose State

// Convert a suspend function to Compose State @Composable fun rememberUserAvatar(userId: String): State<UserImage?> { return produceState<UserImage?>(initialValue = null, userId) { value = api.fetchAvatar(userId) // suspend call → State<T> } } // Convert a callback-based API (e.g., location) to State @Composable fun rememberCurrentLocation(): State<Location?> { val context = LocalContext.current return produceState<Location?>(initialValue = null) { val locationManager = context.getSystemService<LocationManager>()!! val listener = LocationListener { location -> value = location // update Compose state from callback } locationManager.requestLocationUpdates( LocationManager.GPS_PROVIDER, 1000L, 10f, listener ) awaitDispose { locationManager.removeUpdates(listener) // cleanup } } } // Convert connectivity status to State @Composable fun rememberIsOnline(): State<Boolean> { val context = LocalContext.current return produceState(initialValue = true) { val cm = context.getSystemService<ConnectivityManager>()!! val callback = object : ConnectivityManager.NetworkCallback() { override fun onAvailable(network: Network) { value = true } override fun onLost(network: Network) { value = false } } cm.registerDefaultNetworkCallback(callback) awaitDispose { cm.unregisterNetworkCallback(callback) } } }

derivedStateOf — Computed State

// CORRECT: use derivedStateOf for computed values from frequently-changing state @Composable fun MessageList(messages: List<Message>) { val listState = rememberLazyListState() // Without derivedStateOf: recomposes on EVERY scroll pixel // With derivedStateOf: recomposes only when true/false CHANGES val showScrollToTop by remember { derivedStateOf { listState.firstVisibleItemIndex > 5 } } Box { LazyColumn(state = listState) { items(messages, key = { it.id }) { MessageRow(it) } } if (showScrollToTop) { FloatingActionButton(onClick = { /* scroll to top */ }) { Icon(Icons.Default.ArrowUpward, null) } } } }
Common derivedStateOf mistake: Do NOT use derivedStateOf to wrap a Flow.collectAsState(). Flow already produces distinct values. derivedStateOf is for deriving a new value (especially a coarser one) from rapidly-changing State objects like scroll position. If the source state changes at the same rate as the derived value, derivedStateOf adds overhead with no benefit.

rememberUpdatedState — Capturing Latest Values

// Problem: LaunchedEffect(Unit) captures the callback at composition time. // If the callback changes (new lambda), the effect still uses the OLD one. @Composable fun TimedSplash(onTimeout: () -> Unit) { // rememberUpdatedState always holds the LATEST value of onTimeout val currentOnTimeout by rememberUpdatedState(onTimeout) LaunchedEffect(Unit) { // Unit key = runs once, never re-launches delay(3000L) currentOnTimeout() // calls the LATEST onTimeout, not the original } } // Why this matters: // If the parent recomposes and passes a different onTimeout lambda, // without rememberUpdatedState, the LaunchedEffect would still call // the original lambda from first composition.

rememberCoroutineScope — For Event Handlers

// LaunchedEffect runs on composition — but what about onClick? // You can't call suspend functions directly in onClick. // Use rememberCoroutineScope() instead: @Composable fun ScrollToTopButton(listState: LazyListState) { val scope = rememberCoroutineScope() Button(onClick = { scope.launch { listState.animateScrollToItem(0) } // suspend fun in event handler }) { Text("Scroll to top") } }
LaunchedEffect vs rememberCoroutineScope: Use LaunchedEffect for effects tied to composition lifecycle (runs automatically). Use rememberCoroutineScope for effects triggered by user events (onClick, onDrag). Never use rememberCoroutineScope to launch coroutines during composition — that's what LaunchedEffect is for.

CompositionLocal — Implicit Data Passing

CompositionLocal passes data down the composition tree implicitly, without threading it through every function parameter. Think of it like Android's Context — available everywhere without explicit passing.

// Built-in CompositionLocals you use constantly: val context = LocalContext.current // Android Context val density = LocalDensity.current // Screen density val config = LocalConfiguration.current // Screen size, orientation val lifecycleOwner = LocalLifecycleOwner.current val view = LocalView.current // Creating your own CompositionLocal: val LocalUserTheme = compositionLocalOf<UserTheme> { error("No UserTheme provided") // default if not provided } // Providing a value down the tree: CompositionLocalProvider(LocalUserTheme provides darkTheme) { // Everything inside here sees darkTheme MyScreen() // can call LocalUserTheme.current } // Reading the value anywhere in the subtree: @Composable fun ThemedButton() { val theme = LocalUserTheme.current Button(colors = ButtonDefaults.buttonColors(containerColor = theme.primary)) { Text("Themed") } }
compositionLocalOf vs staticCompositionLocalOf: Use compositionLocalOf for values that change (recomposes only readers). Use staticCompositionLocalOf for values that rarely change (recomposes entire subtree on change, but more efficient when stable). Most built-in locals use staticCompositionLocalOf.

Lazy Layouts Deep Dive

Lazy layouts are the Compose equivalent of RecyclerView — they only compose and lay out visible items. The family includes lists, grids, and staggered grids.

LazyColumn and LazyRow

LazyColumn( modifier = Modifier.fillMaxSize(), contentPadding = PaddingValues(16.dp), // padding around all content verticalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp) // spacing between items ) { // Single item — for headers, footers, loading indicators item(key = "header") { Text("Messages", style = MaterialTheme.typography.headlineMedium) } // Sticky header — stays pinned at top during scroll stickyHeader(key = "section-today") { Text( "Today", modifier = Modifier.fillMaxWidth().background(MaterialTheme.colorScheme.surface).padding(8.dp), style = MaterialTheme.typography.labelLarge ) } // items() with key and contentType items( items = todayMessages, key = { it.id }, // CRITICAL for correct state + animations contentType = { it.type } // "text", "image", "header" → better reuse ) { message -> MessageCard(message) } stickyHeader(key = "section-yesterday") { Text("Yesterday", modifier = Modifier.fillMaxWidth().background(MaterialTheme.colorScheme.surface).padding(8.dp)) } // itemsIndexed — when you need the index itemsIndexed( items = yesterdayMessages, key = { _, msg -> msg.id } ) { index, message -> MessageCard(message, showDivider = index < yesterdayMessages.lastIndex) } // Footer loading indicator item(key = "footer") { if (isLoading) CircularProgressIndicator(Modifier.fillMaxWidth().wrapContentWidth()) } }

LazyVerticalGrid and LazyHorizontalGrid

// Fixed number of columns LazyVerticalGrid( columns = GridCells.Fixed(2), // always 2 columns contentPadding = PaddingValues(16.dp), horizontalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp), verticalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp) ) { items(products, key = { it.id }) { product -> ProductCard(product) } } // Adaptive columns — as many as fit, each at least 150dp LazyVerticalGrid( columns = GridCells.Adaptive(minSize = 150.dp) // auto columns based on width ) { items(photos, key = { it.id }, contentType = { "photo" }) { photo -> PhotoThumbnail(photo) } } // GridCells.FixedSize — each cell is exactly this size LazyVerticalGrid( columns = GridCells.FixedSize(120.dp) // each column = exactly 120dp ) { items(items) { ItemCard(it) } } // Spanning full width for headers LazyVerticalGrid(columns = GridCells.Fixed(3)) { item(span = { GridItemSpan(maxLineSpan) }) { // full-width header Text("All Products", style = MaterialTheme.typography.headlineSmall) } items(products, key = { it.id }) { ProductCard(it) } }

LazyVerticalStaggeredGrid

// Staggered grid — items can have different heights (like Pinterest) LazyVerticalStaggeredGrid( columns = StaggeredGridCells.Fixed(2), contentPadding = PaddingValues(16.dp), horizontalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(8.dp), verticalItemSpacing = 8.dp ) { items(pins, key = { it.id }) { pin -> PinCard(pin) // each card can have a different height } } // Adaptive staggered grid LazyVerticalStaggeredGrid( columns = StaggeredGridCells.Adaptive(150.dp) ) { items(photos) { PhotoCard(it) } }

Lazy Layout Performance

Why key matters — Before vs After: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Without key: items(messages) { ... } - Compose identifies items by INDEX - Delete item at index 2 → items 3,4,5... all shift down - State at index 3 now shows for the item that was at index 4 - Checked states, text fields, scroll positions all WRONG - Animations can't track item identity With key: items(messages, key = { it.id }) { ... } - Compose identifies items by UNIQUE KEY - Delete item at index 2 → other items keep their state - Modifier.animateItem() can animate the removal + shift - remember {} inside items is keyed to the item, not position Why contentType matters: - items of the same contentType can reuse each other's composition - Like RecyclerView viewTypes: a "text" item won't try to reuse an "image" item - Improves prefetch efficiency (Compose knows what kind of item is coming next) - Set it when you have heterogeneous items (headers, different card types)
Lazy Layout Performance Tips:
1. Always provide keys — without them, state follows position during scrolling (wrong checkboxes checked).
2. Use contentType — helps Compose reuse compositions between items of the same type.
3. Avoid nesting LazyColumn inside a scrollable Column — use a single LazyColumn with different item types.
4. Keep item composables lightweight — heavy computation in items blocks the UI thread.
5. Use Modifier.animateItem() — animate additions/removals/reorderings (requires keys).
6. Avoid passing mutable lists. Use kotlinx.collections.immutable.ImmutableList for stable parameters that skip recomposition.

Compose Navigation

Jetpack Navigation Compose manages the back stack and screen transitions declaratively:

// Basic NavHost setup val navController = rememberNavController() NavHost(navController, startDestination = "home") { composable("home") { HomeScreen(onItemClick = { id -> navController.navigate("detail/$id") }) } composable( route = "detail/{id}", arguments = listOf(navArgument("id") { type = NavType.IntType }) ) { backStackEntry -> val id = backStackEntry.arguments?.getInt("id") ?: 0 DetailScreen(id) } } // Type-safe navigation (2.8+ with Kotlin Serialization): @Serializable data class Detail(val id: Int) @Serializable data object Home @Serializable data object Settings @Serializable data object Profile NavHost(navController, startDestination = Home) { composable<Home> { HomeScreen() } composable<Detail> { backStackEntry -> val detail: Detail = backStackEntry.toRoute() DetailScreen(detail.id) // compile-time type safety! } }

Nested NavGraphs

// Nested graphs group related destinations and isolate back stacks // Use case: auth flow, onboarding, settings sub-flow @Serializable data object AuthGraph // graph route (not a screen) @Serializable data object Login @Serializable data object Register @Serializable data object ForgotPassword @Serializable data object MainGraph @Serializable data object Home NavHost(navController, startDestination = AuthGraph) { // Auth sub-graph navigation<AuthGraph>(startDestination = Login) { composable<Login> { LoginScreen( onLoginSuccess = { navController.navigate(MainGraph) { popUpTo(AuthGraph) { inclusive = true } // clear auth stack } }, onRegisterClick = { navController.navigate(Register) }, onForgotClick = { navController.navigate(ForgotPassword) } ) } composable<Register> { RegisterScreen() } composable<ForgotPassword> { ForgotPasswordScreen() } } // Main sub-graph navigation<MainGraph>(startDestination = Home) { composable<Home> { HomeScreen() } composable<Settings> { SettingsScreen() } composable<Profile> { ProfileScreen() } } }

Bottom Navigation with Multi-Back-Stack

// Bottom navigation with NavigationBar + multi-back-stack support @Serializable data object HomeTab @Serializable data object SearchTab @Serializable data object ProfileTab data class BottomNavItem( val route: Any, val icon: ImageVector, val label: String ) val tabs = listOf( BottomNavItem(HomeTab, Icons.Default.Home, "Home"), BottomNavItem(SearchTab, Icons.Default.Search, "Search"), BottomNavItem(ProfileTab, Icons.Default.Person, "Profile") ) @Composable fun MainScreen() { val navController = rememberNavController() val navBackStackEntry by navController.currentBackStackEntryAsState() val currentDestination = navBackStackEntry?.destination Scaffold( bottomBar = { NavigationBar { tabs.forEach { tab -> NavigationBarItem( selected = currentDestination?.hierarchy?.any { it.hasRoute(tab.route::class) } == true, onClick = { navController.navigate(tab.route) { // Multi-back-stack: save and restore state per tab popUpTo(navController.graph.findStartDestination().id) { saveState = true } launchSingleTop = true restoreState = true // restore previous tab state } }, icon = { Icon(tab.icon, contentDescription = tab.label) }, label = { Text(tab.label) } ) } } } ) { padding -> NavHost(navController, startDestination = HomeTab, Modifier.padding(padding)) { composable<HomeTab> { HomeScreen() } composable<SearchTab> { SearchScreen() } composable<ProfileTab> { ProfileScreen() } } } }
Multi-back-stack means each tab maintains its own navigation history. When the user switches tabs and comes back, they see the screen they left off on. The key flags are saveState = true on popUpTo and restoreState = true on the navigate call.

Shared Element Transitions (Experimental)

// Shared element transitions animate an element between two screens // Available via navigation-compose 2.8+ (experimental) // In the list screen: SharedTransitionLayout { AnimatedContent(targetState = showDetail) { isDetail -> if (!isDetail) { // List item with shared element Image( painter = painterResource(R.drawable.photo), contentDescription = null, modifier = Modifier .sharedElement( state = rememberSharedContentState(key = "photo-$id"), animatedVisibilityScope = this@AnimatedContent ) .size(80.dp) .clip(RoundedCornerShape(8.dp)) ) } else { // Detail screen with same shared element Image( painter = painterResource(R.drawable.photo), contentDescription = null, modifier = Modifier .sharedElement( state = rememberSharedContentState(key = "photo-$id"), animatedVisibilityScope = this@AnimatedContent ) .fillMaxWidth() .height(300.dp) ) } } }

Animations — Complete Guide

Compose makes animations declarative — describe the target state, Compose handles the transition. There is an animation API for every use case.

animate*AsState — Animate a Single Value

// animateFloatAsState — smoothly animate a float value val alpha by animateFloatAsState( targetValue = if (isSelected) 1f else 0.3f, animationSpec = tween(durationMillis = 300) // duration-based ) Text("Fading", modifier = Modifier.alpha(alpha)) // animateColorAsState — smoothly transition between colors val backgroundColor by animateColorAsState( targetValue = if (isError) Color.Red else Color.Green, animationSpec = tween(500) ) Box(Modifier.size(100.dp).background(backgroundColor)) // animateDpAsState — animate dimension values val cornerRadius by animateDpAsState( targetValue = if (isExpanded) 0.dp else 16.dp, animationSpec = spring( dampingRatio = Spring.DampingRatioMediumBouncy, // bouncy spring stiffness = Spring.StiffnessLow ) ) Card(shape = RoundedCornerShape(cornerRadius)) { /* content */ } // animateIntAsState, animateOffsetAsState, animateSizeAsState, etc. val elevation by animateDpAsState( targetValue = if (isPressed) 2.dp else 8.dp, animationSpec = spring(stiffness = Spring.StiffnessMedium) )
AnimationSpec types: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ tween(durationMillis, delayMillis, easing) → Duration-based. Easing: FastOutSlowInEasing, LinearEasing, etc. spring(dampingRatio, stiffness) → Physics-based. DampingRatioNoBouncy (1f), DampingRatioLowBouncy (0.75f), DampingRatioMediumBouncy (0.5f), DampingRatioHighBouncy (0.2f) StiffnessHigh (10000f), StiffnessMedium (1500f), StiffnessLow (200f) keyframes { durationMillis = 1000; 0f at 0; 0.5f at 500 using LinearEasing; 1f at 1000 } → Define exact values at specific times repeatable(iterations, animation, repeatMode) → Repeat a finite number of times (RepeatMode.Reverse or Restart) infiniteRepeatable(animation, repeatMode) → Repeat forever snap(delayMillis) → Instant jump, no animation

AnimatedVisibility — Show/Hide with Animation

// Basic AnimatedVisibility with custom enter/exit AnimatedVisibility( visible = isVisible, enter = fadeIn(animationSpec = tween(300)) + slideInVertically(initialOffsetY = { -it }), // slide from top exit = fadeOut(animationSpec = tween(200)) + slideOutVertically(targetOffsetY = { it }) // slide to bottom ) { Card { Text("Animated card", Modifier.padding(16.dp)) } } // All EnterTransition types: // fadeIn() — fade from transparent // slideInVertically() / slideInHorizontally() — slide in // expandIn() / expandVertically() / expandHorizontally() — grow from nothing // scaleIn() — scale from small // All ExitTransition types: // fadeOut() — fade to transparent // slideOutVertically() / slideOutHorizontally() — slide out // shrinkOut() / shrinkVertically() / shrinkHorizontally() — shrink to nothing // scaleOut() — scale to small // Combine with + operator: enter = fadeIn() + expandVertically(expandFrom = Alignment.Top) exit = fadeOut() + shrinkVertically(shrinkTowards = Alignment.Top) // animateEnterExit inside AnimatedVisibility scope AnimatedVisibility(visible = isVisible) { Column { Text("Always uses parent animation") Text( "Custom child animation", modifier = Modifier.animateEnterExit( enter = slideInHorizontally(), exit = slideOutHorizontally() ) ) } }

AnimatedContent — Animate Content Switching

// AnimatedContent animates between different composable content AnimatedContent( targetState = count, transitionSpec = { // Define how old content exits and new content enters if (targetState > initialState) { // Counting up: slide up + fade in, slide up + fade out (slideInVertically { it } + fadeIn()).togetherWith( slideOutVertically { -it } + fadeOut() ) } else { // Counting down: slide down (slideInVertically { -it } + fadeIn()).togetherWith( slideOutVertically { it } + fadeOut() ) }.using(SizeTransform(clip = false)) // animate size changes }, label = "counter" ) { targetCount -> Text( "$targetCount", style = MaterialTheme.typography.displayLarge ) } // Simpler: tab content switching with AnimatedContent AnimatedContent( targetState = selectedTab, label = "tab-content" ) { tab -> when (tab) { Tab.Feed -> FeedContent() Tab.Explore -> ExploreContent() Tab.Profile -> ProfileContent() } }

Crossfade — Simple Content Switching

// Crossfade: simple crossfade between composables based on state // Simpler than AnimatedContent — only supports crossfade, no slide/scale Crossfade( targetState = currentTab, animationSpec = tween(400), label = "tab-crossfade" ) { tab -> when (tab) { Tab.Home -> HomeScreen() Tab.Settings -> SettingsScreen() } }

animateContentSize — Auto-Animate Size Changes

// Automatically animates when a composable's size changes Column( modifier = Modifier .animateContentSize( animationSpec = spring( dampingRatio = Spring.DampingRatioLowBouncy, stiffness = Spring.StiffnessLow ) ) .padding(16.dp) ) { Text("Title", style = MaterialTheme.typography.titleMedium) if (expanded) { Spacer(Modifier.height(8.dp)) Text("This is the expanded content that appears with a smooth size animation.") Text("Additional details shown when expanded.") } }

updateTransition — Coordinated Animations

// updateTransition coordinates multiple animations around a single state enum class CardState { Normal, Selected, Pressed } @Composable fun AnimatedCard(state: CardState) { val transition = updateTransition(targetState = state, label = "card") val borderColor by transition.animateColor(label = "border") { targetState -> when (targetState) { CardState.Normal -> Color.Gray CardState.Selected -> Color.Blue CardState.Pressed -> Color.DarkGray } } val elevation by transition.animateDp(label = "elevation") { targetState -> when (targetState) { CardState.Normal -> 2.dp CardState.Selected -> 8.dp CardState.Pressed -> 0.dp } } val scale by transition.animateFloat(label = "scale") { targetState -> when (targetState) { CardState.Normal -> 1f CardState.Selected -> 1.05f CardState.Pressed -> 0.95f } } Card( modifier = Modifier .graphicsLayer { scaleX = scale; scaleY = scale } .border(2.dp, borderColor, RoundedCornerShape(12.dp)), elevation = CardDefaults.cardElevation(defaultElevation = elevation) ) { Text("Animated Card", Modifier.padding(24.dp)) } }

InfiniteTransition — Repeating Animations

// InfiniteTransition for animations that loop forever @Composable fun PulsingDot() { val infiniteTransition = rememberInfiniteTransition(label = "pulse") val scale by infiniteTransition.animateFloat( initialValue = 0.8f, targetValue = 1.2f, animationSpec = infiniteRepeatable( animation = tween(600, easing = FastOutSlowInEasing), repeatMode = RepeatMode.Reverse // ping-pong ), label = "scale" ) val alpha by infiniteTransition.animateFloat( initialValue = 0.4f, targetValue = 1f, animationSpec = infiniteRepeatable( animation = tween(600), repeatMode = RepeatMode.Reverse ), label = "alpha" ) Box( modifier = Modifier .size(16.dp) .graphicsLayer { scaleX = scale; scaleY = scale; this.alpha = alpha } .background(Color.Red, CircleShape) ) } // Loading spinner with infinite rotation @Composable fun LoadingSpinner() { val infiniteTransition = rememberInfiniteTransition(label = "spin") val angle by infiniteTransition.animateFloat( initialValue = 0f, targetValue = 360f, animationSpec = infiniteRepeatable( animation = tween(1000, easing = LinearEasing), repeatMode = RepeatMode.Restart ), label = "rotation" ) Icon( Icons.Default.Refresh, "Loading", modifier = Modifier.graphicsLayer { rotationZ = angle } ) }

Gesture + Animation

// Swipe-to-dismiss with animation snap-back @Composable fun SwipeToDismissItem( onDismiss: () -> Unit, content: @Composable () -> Unit ) { val offsetX = remember { Animatable(0f) } val scope = rememberCoroutineScope() Box( modifier = Modifier .offset { IntOffset(offsetX.value.roundToInt(), 0) } .pointerInput(Unit) { detectHorizontalDragGestures( onDragEnd = { scope.launch { if (abs(offsetX.value) > size.width * 0.4f) { // Swiped far enough → dismiss offsetX.animateTo( targetValue = size.width.toFloat() * sign(offsetX.value), animationSpec = tween(200) ) onDismiss() } else { // Not far enough → snap back with spring offsetX.animateTo( targetValue = 0f, animationSpec = spring( dampingRatio = Spring.DampingRatioMediumBouncy ) ) } } } ) { _, dragAmount -> scope.launch { offsetX.snapTo(offsetX.value + dragAmount) } } } ) { content() } } // Draggable with spring snap to positions @Composable fun DraggableHandle() { val offsetY = remember { Animatable(0f) } val scope = rememberCoroutineScope() Box( Modifier .offset { IntOffset(0, offsetY.value.roundToInt()) } .draggable( state = rememberDraggableState { delta -> scope.launch { offsetY.snapTo(offsetY.value + delta) } }, orientation = Orientation.Vertical, onDragStopped = { scope.launch { // Snap to nearest position: 0, 200, or 400 val snapPositions = listOf(0f, 200f, 400f) val nearest = snapPositions.minBy { abs(it - offsetY.value) } offsetY.animateTo(nearest, spring(stiffness = Spring.StiffnessMedium)) } } ) .size(48.dp) .background(Color.Blue, RoundedCornerShape(8.dp)) ) }
Animation Decision Tree: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Show/hide content? → AnimatedVisibility Animate a single value? → animate*AsState (Float, Color, Dp, Int, etc.) Size changes? → Modifier.animateContentSize() Swap content (simple)? → Crossfade Swap content (custom)? → AnimatedContent (with transitionSpec) Multiple coordinated? → updateTransition Infinite/repeating? → rememberInfiniteTransition Gesture-driven? → Animatable + pointerInput/draggable Full custom control? → Animatable + LaunchedEffect

Window Insets and Edge-to-Edge

Modern Android apps draw behind the system bars (status bar, navigation bar) for an immersive experience. Compose provides the WindowInsets API to handle this correctly.

// Step 1: Enable edge-to-edge in Activity.onCreate() class MainActivity : ComponentActivity() { override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?) { super.onCreate(savedInstanceState) enableEdgeToEdge() // draws behind status bar and nav bar setContent { MyTheme { MyApp() } } } } // Step 2: Handle insets in your composables @Composable fun MyApp() { Scaffold( modifier = Modifier.fillMaxSize(), topBar = { TopAppBar( title = { Text("My App") }, modifier = Modifier.statusBarsPadding() // OR handled by Scaffold ) } ) { innerPadding -> // Scaffold's innerPadding already accounts for system bars LazyColumn(contentPadding = innerPadding) { items(items) { ItemCard(it) } } } }
// WindowInsets types available in Compose: WindowInsets.systemBars // status bar + navigation bar WindowInsets.statusBars // just the status bar WindowInsets.navigationBars // just the navigation bar (bottom/side) WindowInsets.ime // software keyboard WindowInsets.displayCutout // notch/punch-hole WindowInsets.safeContent // combination: system bars + display cutout + IME WindowInsets.safeDrawing // safe area for drawing // Modifier-based inset padding: Modifier.statusBarsPadding() // pad for status bar Modifier.navigationBarsPadding() // pad for nav bar Modifier.systemBarsPadding() // pad for both Modifier.imePadding() // pad for keyboard Modifier.safeContentPadding() // pad for all safe insets Modifier.windowInsetsPadding(WindowInsets.systemBars) // explicit // Animating with the keyboard (IME): @Composable fun ChatInput() { TextField( value = text, onValueChange = { text = it }, modifier = Modifier .fillMaxWidth() .imePadding() // moves up smoothly when keyboard appears ) }
Scaffold + insets: Scaffold automatically consumes system bar insets for its topBar and bottomBar slots. The innerPadding passed to the content lambda includes these insets. If you apply systemBarsPadding() and use innerPadding, you will get double padding. Use one or the other.

View <-> Compose Interop

In real apps, you migrate incrementally. Compose and Views must coexist:

// Compose inside a View (Fragment/Activity): class MyFragment : Fragment() { override fun onCreateView(...) = ComposeView(requireContext()).apply { setViewCompositionStrategy( ViewCompositionStrategy.DisposeOnViewTreeLifecycleDestroyed ) setContent { MaterialTheme { MyComposableScreen() } } } } // View inside Compose (wrapping legacy Views): @Composable fun LegacyMapView() { AndroidView( factory = { context -> MapView(context).apply { onCreate(null) } }, update = { mapView -> mapView.moveCamera(...) } // called on recomposition ) }
ViewCompositionStrategy controls when Compose disposes. Use DisposeOnViewTreeLifecycleDestroyed for Fragments (disposes when Fragment view is destroyed). The default DisposeOnDetachedFromWindow can cause issues in ViewPager2 or Fragment transitions.

Recomposition Performance

Compose can skip recomposition of a composable when all parameters are stable and unchanged. Understanding stability is key to performance:

Stable types (Compose can skip recomposition): ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ✓ Primitives: Int, Float, Boolean, String ✓ data class with only stable fields ✓ @Immutable classes (promise: never changes) ✓ @Stable classes (promise: changes are observable) ✓ Function types (lambdas — if captured values are stable) Unstable types (Compose ALWAYS recomposes): ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ ✗ List, Set, Map (mutable interfaces) ✗ Classes from external modules (Compose can't verify) ✗ data class with any unstable field Fix: Use @Immutable annotation or kotlinx.collections.immutable

Infinite Scroll & Auto-Scroll Patterns

Infinite scrolling (pagination) and auto-scrolling (chat) are two of the most common list patterns in production apps. Compose handles both elegantly.

Infinite Scroll — Manual Implementation

// Step 1: Detect when user is near the end of the list @Composable fun InfiniteScrollList(viewModel: FeedViewModel) { val items by viewModel.items.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() val isLoading by viewModel.isLoading.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() val listState = rememberLazyListState() // derivedStateOf: only recomposes when the boolean CHANGES, // not on every scroll pixel val shouldLoadMore by remember { derivedStateOf { val lastVisibleItem = listState.layoutInfo.visibleItemsInfo.lastOrNull() ?: return@derivedStateOf false lastVisibleItem.index >= listState.layoutInfo.totalItemsCount - 5 // trigger when within 5 items of the end } } // Step 2: Trigger loadMore when near end LaunchedEffect(shouldLoadMore) { if (shouldLoadMore && !isLoading) { viewModel.loadMore() } } // Step 3: LazyColumn with loading footer LazyColumn(state = listState) { items(items, key = { it.id }) { item -> FeedItemCard(item) } // Loading footer if (isLoading) { item(key = "loading") { Box( Modifier.fillMaxWidth().padding(16.dp), contentAlignment = Alignment.Center ) { CircularProgressIndicator() } } } } }

Infinite Scroll — Paging 3 Library

For production apps, Jetpack Paging 3 handles pagination, caching, error/retry, and placeholders out of the box:

// 1. Define a PagingSource class ArticlePagingSource(private val api: ArticleApi) : PagingSource<Int, Article>() { override suspend fun load(params: LoadParams<Int>): LoadResult<Int, Article> { val page = params.key ?: 1 return try { val response = api.getArticles(page, params.loadSize) LoadResult.Page( data = response.articles, prevKey = if (page == 1) null else page - 1, nextKey = if (response.articles.isEmpty()) null else page + 1 ) } catch (e: Exception) { LoadResult.Error(e) } } override fun getRefreshKey(state: PagingState<Int, Article>) = state.anchorPosition?.let { state.closestPageToPosition(it)?.prevKey?.plus(1) } } // 2. Create Pager in ViewModel class ArticleViewModel : ViewModel() { val articles: Flow<PagingData<Article>> = Pager( config = PagingConfig(pageSize = 20, prefetchDistance = 5) ) { ArticlePagingSource(api) }.flow.cachedIn(viewModelScope) } // 3. Collect in Compose — automatic loading when near end @Composable fun ArticleList(viewModel: ArticleViewModel) { val articles = viewModel.articles.collectAsLazyPagingItems() LazyColumn { items( count = articles.itemCount, key = articles.itemKey { it.id } ) { index -> val article = articles[index] // accessing triggers auto-load! article?.let { ArticleCard(it) } } } }
Paging 3 magic: When you call articles[index] near the end of loaded data, Paging automatically fetches the next page. No manual scroll detection needed. PagingConfig.prefetchDistance controls how far ahead to start loading (default = pageSize).

Auto-Scroll to Bottom — Chat Pattern

// Approach 1: LaunchedEffect + animateScrollToItem @Composable fun ChatScreen(viewModel: ChatViewModel) { val messages by viewModel.messages.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() val listState = rememberLazyListState() // Auto-scroll when new messages arrive LaunchedEffect(messages.size) { if (messages.isNotEmpty()) { listState.animateScrollToItem(messages.lastIndex) } } LazyColumn(state = listState) { items(messages, key = { it.id }) { msg -> MessageBubble(msg) } } } // Approach 2: reverseLayout — newest at bottom, no auto-scroll needed @Composable fun ChatScreenReversed(viewModel: ChatViewModel) { val messages by viewModel.messages.collectAsStateWithLifecycle() LazyColumn( reverseLayout = true // items stack from bottom ) { // Pass items in REVERSE order since layout is reversed items(messages.reversed(), key = { it.id }) { msg -> MessageBubble(msg) } } // New items appear at bottom automatically — no scroll logic needed! // User's scroll position is preserved when new messages arrive. }
reverseLayout = true is the preferred chat pattern. The list starts at the bottom and scrolls up. New items added to the end of the data appear at the bottom without any auto-scroll code. The user's reading position is preserved — they won't be jerked to the bottom when they are scrolling up through history.

ImmutableList + Incremental Updates with key

A common misconception: "Setting a new list means LazyColumn recomposes ALL items." With proper key, LazyColumn is as efficient as RecyclerView's DiffUtil — it skips unchanged items and only composes new ones.

With key: insert [D,E] into [A,B,C]: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Old list: [A, B, C] New list: [A, B, C, D, E] LazyColumn with key = { it.id }: A → key "a" matches → SKIPPED (not recomposed) B → key "b" matches → SKIPPED C → key "c" matches → SKIPPED D → key "d" is NEW → COMPOSED ✨ E → key "e" is NEW → COMPOSED ✨ Like RecyclerView notifyItemRangeInserted(3, 2) Without key: insert [D,E] into [A,B,C]: ━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━━ Old list: [A, B, C] New list: [A, B, C, D, E] LazyColumn WITHOUT key: Index 0 → still A, but Compose doesn't KNOW that → RECOMPOSED Index 1 → still B → RECOMPOSED Index 2 → still C → RECOMPOSED Index 3 → new D → COMPOSED Index 4 → new E → COMPOSED Like RecyclerView notifyDataSetChanged() — everything refreshes
// The KEY insight: you DO set a new list, but only NEW items compose class ChatViewModel : ViewModel() { private val _messages = MutableStateFlow<ImmutableList<Message>>(persistentListOf()) val messages: StateFlow<ImmutableList<Message>> = _messages.asStateFlow() fun onNewMessage(msg: Message) { _messages.update { currentList -> // PersistentList.add() returns a NEW list using structural sharing // Internally: O(log n) — reuses most of the tree, copies only the path currentList.add(msg) } } } // In the UI — key makes the difference @Composable fun ChatMessages(messages: ImmutableList<Message>) { LazyColumn { items(messages, key = { it.id }) { msg -> // Only NEW messages trigger composition // Existing messages with same key + same @Stable inputs = SKIPPED MessageBubble(msg) } } }
PersistentList from kotlinx.collections.immutable: Unlike toList() which copies the entire list (O(n)), PersistentList.add() uses structural sharing — it reuses the existing tree structure and only allocates nodes along the modified path (O(log n)). For a 10,000-item list, adding one item copies ~13 nodes instead of 10,000 elements. Use persistentListOf() or .toPersistentList() to create one.

RecyclerView vs LazyColumn — Operations Comparison

RecyclerViewLazyColumn EquivalentHow
notifyItemRangeInsertedSet new list + keyAutomatic — only new keys compose
DiffUtilAutomatic with keyCompose diffs by key internally
notifyItemChanged with payloadRecompose skips if @Stable inputs sameCompose's smart recomposition
notifyDataSetChangedSet list WITHOUT keyAll items recompose (avoid this)
notifyItemMovedSet new list + key + animateItem()Compose tracks identity by key
ViewHolder viewTypecontentTypeBetter composition reuse
The #1 LazyColumn performance mistake: Forgetting key. Without keys, LazyColumn behaves like notifyDataSetChanged() — every visible item recomposes on any list change. With keys, it behaves like DiffUtil — only changed/new items recompose. Always provide key = { it.id } for any list that changes.

questions.title: LazyColumn Item Reuse Bug
questions.description: This LazyColumn shows a list of messages, but when you scroll, the wrong items show checked states. Fix the issue.

checks.message: Add a key parameter to items() to uniquely identify each item

questions.explanation: Without a key, LazyColumn associates remember state with position, not identity. When items are recycled during scrolling, state stays at the position. Adding key = { it.id } binds state to each message's unique ID.
hints: When items move or recycle, how does Compose know which state belongs to which item?

questions.title: Unnecessary Recomposition
questions.description: This composable recomposes the entire list every time the timestamp updates (every second). Optimize it so only the timestamp text recomposes.

checks.message: Extract the timestamp into a separate composable

questions.explanation: When currentTime changes, Compose recomposes any composable reading it. By extracting the timestamp into its own composable, only TimestampHeader recomposes. This is the principle of narrow recomposition scope.
hints: Which state is changing frequently? Which part of the UI needs it?
Compose recomposes at the composable function boundary

questions.title: LaunchedEffect
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to show a snackbar when an error occurs.

blanks.hint: Side effect that launches a coroutine
Key that triggers re-launch when changed

questions.explanation: LaunchedEffect(key) launches a coroutine when entering composition and re-launches when the key changes. showSnackbar() is a suspend function, requiring the coroutine scope.
hints: This side effect runs a coroutine tied to composition lifecycle

questions.title: Side Effects in Compose
questions.description: Which side effect API should you use to register and unregister a broadcast receiver while the composable is in the composition?
options: LaunchedEffect
DisposableEffect
SideEffect
rememberCoroutineScope
questions.explanation: DisposableEffect is designed for effects needing cleanup — it provides an onDispose block. Perfect for register/unregister patterns. LaunchedEffect is for coroutines. SideEffect runs after every recomposition with no cleanup.
hints: Which effect gives you a cleanup callback?

questions.title: derivedStateOf
questions.description: What does derivedStateOf do in this code?
options: Caches computation and only triggers recomposition when its result changes (true/false), not on every scroll pixel
Runs the computation on a background thread
Same as remember{} — just caches the initial value
Delays computation until layout is complete
questions.explanation: derivedStateOf creates derived state that only triggers recomposition when its result changes. Without it, reading scroll position directly would recompose on every pixel. With it, the boolean only changes when crossing the threshold, dramatically reducing recompositions.
hints: Think about how often scroll position changes vs how often the boolean changes

questions.title: AnimatedVisibility
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to animate a panel sliding in and out based on a boolean state.
questions.explanation: AnimatedVisibility animates the appearance and disappearance of its content based on a boolean. By default it uses fade + expand animations. You can customize with enter and exit parameters.
hints: This composable handles the animation automatically based on a boolean
The boolean state controls whether content is shown or hidden

questions.title: DisposableEffect Missing Cleanup
questions.description: This code registers a lifecycle observer but never removes it, causing a memory leak. Fix it.

checks.message: Clean up the observer in onDispose

questions.explanation: LaunchedEffect provides no cleanup mechanism. DisposableEffect provides onDispose { } which runs when the composable leaves composition or the key changes. Always remove observers, unregister receivers, and close resources in onDispose.
hints: LaunchedEffect is for coroutines, not register/unregister patterns
What happens to the observer when this composable is removed from the tree?

questions.title: Composition over Inheritance
questions.description: Why does Jetpack Compose use composition (small composable functions) instead of inheritance (extending View classes)?
options: Composition is faster at runtime
Composition allows mixing and matching behaviors freely without rigid class hierarchies
Composition uses less memory
Composition was easier for Google to implement
questions.explanation: Inheritance creates rigid hierarchies — you can't have a Button that's also a TextField. Composition lets you freely combine behaviors: wrap any content in any container, add any modifier to any composable. A Button is just a composable that takes content as a lambda — the content can be anything. This is far more flexible than the old View class hierarchy.
hints: Think about the old View system: Button extends TextView extends View — what if you want something that doesn't fit?

questions.title: Custom Layout Composable
questions.description: Fill in the blanks to create a custom layout that places all children in a vertical stack (like a simplified Column).

blanks.hint: The composable used to create custom layouts
Call this on a Measurable to get a Placeable
Defines the layout size and contains placement logic
Positions the placeable at x, y coordinates

questions.explanation: The Layout composable is the foundation for custom layouts. Each child (Measurable) is measured once to produce a Placeable. The layout(width, height) block sets the final size and uses placeRelative(x, y) to position each child. placeRelative is RTL-aware (mirrors coordinates in RTL locales).
hints: The Layout composable takes content, modifier, and a measure policy lambda
Measurables are measured to produce Placeables, which are then placed

questions.title: LazyVerticalGrid Column Types
questions.description: You want a photo grid where columns are at least 120dp wide, and the grid automatically adds more columns on wider screens. Which GridCells configuration should you use?
options: GridCells.Fixed(3)
GridCells.Adaptive(minSize = 120.dp)
GridCells.FixedSize(120.dp)
GridCells.Fixed(120)
questions.explanation: GridCells.Adaptive(minSize = 120.dp) calculates the maximum number of columns that fit, with each being at least 120dp. On a 360dp-wide phone, you get 3 columns (120dp each). On a 600dp tablet, you get 5 columns. GridCells.Fixed(3) always shows 3 columns regardless of screen width. GridCells.FixedSize creates columns of exactly 120dp, wasting remaining space.
hints: Which configuration adapts to different screen widths automatically?

questions.title: updateTransition Animation
questions.description: What does updateTransition achieve that individual animate*AsState calls do not?
options: Coordinates all three animations to start, run, and finish together as a single transition
Runs the animations on a background thread for better performance
Automatically reverses the animations when the state changes back
Reduces memory usage by sharing a single animation instance
questions.explanation: updateTransition groups multiple animations around a single state change, ensuring they are coordinated — they start simultaneously and the transition is considered complete only when all child animations finish. With separate animate*AsState calls, each animation runs independently with no coordination guarantee. updateTransition also works with Compose tooling (Animation Preview) to inspect all animations together.
hints: Think about what 'coordinated' means — do individual animate*AsState calls know about each other?

questions.title: Slot API Composable Bug
questions.description: This custom composable has a slot API design problem. The actions slot content does not get arranged horizontally. Fix it so the caller's actions appear in a row.

checks.message: Wrap the actions slot in a Row or use RowScope receiver

questions.explanation: The actions slot should either be wrapped in a Row or use a RowScope receiver so that multiple children arrange horizontally. Using RowScope.() -> Unit as the slot type lets callers use weight() and other Row-specific modifiers. The title uses Modifier.weight(1f) to push actions to the end.
hints: How do the actions know they should be laid out horizontally?
Consider the slot's lambda receiver type

questions.title: Infinite Scroll Detection
questions.description: In a manual infinite scroll implementation, why should you wrap the scroll-position check in derivedStateOf instead of reading listState.layoutInfo directly in the composable body?
options: derivedStateOf runs the check on a background thread
derivedStateOf caches the result and only triggers recomposition when the boolean result changes (near-end or not), rather than on every scroll pixel
derivedStateOf is required by the LazyListState API
derivedStateOf prevents the LazyColumn from being recomposed entirely
questions.explanation: Scroll position changes on every pixel of scroll, which would cause constant recomposition if read directly. derivedStateOf computes a derived value (a boolean: "are we near the end?") and only triggers recomposition when that result changes — from false to true or vice versa. This dramatically reduces unnecessary recompositions from hundreds-per-scroll to just 1-2 threshold crossings.
hints: How often does scroll position change versus how often do you need to trigger a load?
derivedStateOf is about converting a fine-grained signal into a coarse-grained one

questions.title: Key Behavior with List Updates
questions.description: A LazyColumn displays items [A, B, C] with key = { it.id }. The ViewModel sets a new list [A, B, C, D, E]. What does LazyColumn do?
options: Recomposes all 5 items (A through E) since a new list was set
Skips A, B, C (keys match and @Stable inputs unchanged) and only composes D and E — like RecyclerView's notifyItemRangeInserted
Throws an exception because the list reference changed
Removes all items and recreates them from scratch
questions.explanation: With key = { it.id }, Compose identifies each item by its unique key. When the new list arrives, Compose sees that keys A, B, C already exist with unchanged @Stable inputs — so it skips recomposing them entirely. Only D and E are new keys, so only those two get composed. This is equivalent to notifyItemRangeInserted(3, 2) in RecyclerView. Without key, ALL visible items would recompose (like notifyDataSetChanged).
hints: Think about how Compose uses keys to track item identity across list updates
Compare: what would happen WITHOUT the key parameter?
